data.title: The Early Innings: Why Japan's Structural Story Has Years To Run
data.description:
By Daisuke Nomoto, CMA (SAAJ), Senior Portfolio Manager | Simon Morton-Grant, Client Portfolio Manager

Japan's economic renaissance has captured global attention, but skeptics continue to ask: How sustainable is this transformation? As we enter 2026, this question has evolved into something more fundamental: Are we witnessing a temporary market cycle or the early stages of a structural transformation that could reshape Japan for decades? Evidence is increasingly pointing towards the latter. What began as a governance-driven rally in 2023 has revealed itself as something far more profound — a convergence of forces that mirrors the early stages of America's great wealth creation cycle of the 1980s.

The governance revolution: Still in its infancy

Across boardrooms in Tokyo, the conversation has fundamentally changed from cash retention and stability to an urgent focus on shareholder returns and capital efficiency.

The numbers tell the story of a transformation gathering momentum. Japanese companies returned 60% of net income to shareholders in 2025, yet they are still sitting on the cash equivalent to 40% of GDP — more than double the U.S. ratio. This isn't a problem to be solved overnight; it is a multi-year opportunity that will systematically unlock value as companies learn to deploy capital more efficiently. We are already seeing evidence of this shift. Share buybacks have multiplied nine times since 2012, cross-shareholdings continue to unwind and dividends have doubled in 10 years. These are not temporary measures driven by market pressure — they represent a fundamental shift in how Japanese business operates. The revolution has begun, but we are still in the early chapters.

Multiple catalysts align for sustained growth

Governance reform alone doesn't explain the timing of this renaissance. Three powerful catalysts are converging to create a ‘why now’ moment that makes this transformation particularly compelling.

1. Significant shift from a savings to investment culture. Japanese households are finally awakening from decades of financial dormancy. Sitting on $14 trillion in assets (nearly five times France's entire GDP), half of which is languishing in cash, millions of savers are beginning to invest against a backdrop of persistent inflation. Improved NISA accounts (Japan's tax-free investment scheme) are accelerating this shift from savings to investment, securing sustained equity inflows for years ahead.

2. The transition from deflation to inflation is reshaping consumption and investment patterns across both corporate and household sectors. Nominal GDP growth is projecting upward, and the TOPIX historically tracks this trajectory. Crucially, this growth stems from robust domestic demand — a marked departure from Japan's export-dependent recovery patterns of the past.

3. Japan's political winds have shifted decisively. Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's administration builds on Abenomics but charts a new course. Where Abe aimed to defeat deflation and restore growth with monetary and fiscal policies, Takaichi pursues ‘Sanaenomics’ — a strategy that fuses economic growth with national security under the banner of Economic Security and Strategic Investment. This isn't just policy evolution; it is a fundamental reimagining of Japan's global positioning. With fiscal and monetary support, the government is partnering with private industry to dominate tomorrow's critical technologies: artificial intelligence (AI), semiconductors, quantum computing, shipbuilding, cybersecurity and digital content. The momentum is building, and Japan is positioning itself to reclaim a place towards the world's economic summit.

What makes this convergence particularly compelling is that Japanese valuations remain attractive despite recent gains. The TOPIX is currently trading at a fraction of its bubble peak of 64x price/earnings, with more than a third of index companies still below book value. Relative to U.S. markets, Japanese equities trade at a significant discount. This positions Japan as a natural beneficiary of any rotation away from U.S. exceptionalism.

Japan's investment landscape transforms

Japan's investment story is evolving beyond its traditional automation and digital transformation themes. The new administration's strategic vision, combined with structural shifts, is unlocking opportunities across unexpected sectors.

  • AI adoption. As AI reshapes global markets, Japan faces a unique opportunity. With its shrinking workforce, AI adoption addresses productivity challenges without the job displacement fears plaguing other nations. This demographic reality transforms potential disruption into competitive advantage.
  • Infrastructure spend. Japan's economic miracle left behind a legacy that now demands attention. Infrastructure built 40–50 years ago during the high-growth and bubble eras requires comprehensive renovation. This isn't cyclical demand, it's a structural necessity. Construction companies stand to benefit from decades of sustained activity renovating buildings, upgrading facilities and rebuilding aging transportation networks.
  • Fan culture. This market segment has quietly grown into an economic force in Japan. ‘Oshikatsu’ — supporting favorite idols, anime characters, VTubers and athletes — now rivals the global anime industry in market size, revealing untapped commercial potential in Japan's cultural exports.
  • Refloating shipbuilding. Geopolitical tensions are reviving an ancient truth: Maritime control shapes global power. The Takaichi administration has designated shipbuilding as strategic, promising comprehensive public/private support as defense and maritime equipment sectors benefit from rising international instability.
  • Osaka’s reemergence. Once ‘the nation’s kitchen’ (Japan’s main hub for rice trade and economic activity), Osaka lost ground to Tokyo after the 1990’s bubble burst. Now, with the Ishin Party's coalition influence, momentum builds behind Osaka's subcapital vision. Major projects such as the Integrated Resort, the Osaka Station redevelopment and the Hokuriku Shinkansen extension signal economic revival ahead.

The wealth creation playbook: Japan today mirrors 1980s U.S.

The parallels to America's transformation in the early 1980s are striking and instructive. Back then, 40% of U.S. stocks traded below book value — almost identical to Japan today. American companies were directionless after the stagflation of the 1970s, just as Japanese firms remained shell-shocked decades after their bubble burst.

The catalyst in both cases was the same: A corporate awakening focused on shareholder returns. Ronald Reagan's deregulation and the shift towards M&A-driven restructuring launched the greatest wealth creation cycle in modern history. Japan is following an eerily similar script, with governance reforms systematically pushing return on equity (ROE) higher across the market.

The retail investor revolution also provides another compelling parallel. Equity and investment trusts comprised just 36% of U.S. household allocation in 1985, growing to 55% today. Japan currently sits at 19% — way below the 1985 U.S. level — and the trajectory is clear. With 50% of assets still in cash, the potential for reallocation into equities dwarfs what America experienced four decades ago. Japan's NISA accounts are following a similar transformative path that 401(k)s and IRAs carved in the U.S., potentially unlocking trillions in household savings for equity investment.

The active advantage: Navigating inefficiency

Japan's transformation demands an active investment approach. The evidence is clear: The median active Japan equity manager outperforms the MSCI Japan index over every time period in the past decade. Active managers are well positioned to create genuine alpha opportunities from a market riddled with inefficiencies.

Japan has 4,000 listed companies, yet only 35% have a return on equity above 10%. This means the majority languish at or below their cost of capital. For passive investors, this creates an uncomfortable reality: They are forced to own underperforming companies simply because they exist in the index. Active management enables investment in attractively priced, high-quality companies, as well as in names poised for transformation. Furthermore, more than 60% of these Japanese companies are covered by one sell-side analyst or none at all, compared to 70% of U.S. small caps that each attract three or more analysts. This research vacuum is a treasure map for active managers willing to dig deeper. While the market sleeps on corporate Japan's transformation, skilled managers are unearthing tomorrow's winners.

Our quality core investment approach strategically targets these opportunities. We focus on exceptional, inexpensive businesses with strong fundamentals and management teams dedicated to unlocking shareholder value. This methodology has consistently delivered alpha throughout Japan's transformation.

The bottom line: Japan's defining investment moment

Japan's renaissance isn't a cyclical upturn; it is a structural transformation that could span decades. The convergence of corporate governance reform, demographic-driven capital reallocation, political realignment and attractive valuations creates a compelling multi-year investment thesis.

Like the U.S. in the 1980s, Japan stands at the threshold of a generational wealth creation cycle. But we are still in the early innings. The governance revolution is accelerating, household capital is just beginning to mobilize and political momentum favors continued reform.

The structural drivers remain intact, the runway for reform extends years ahead and the opportunity for active managers to generate alpha has rarely been more compelling. We believe Japan's renaissance is just beginning — and those who recognize its structural nature today will be best positioned to benefit from the transformation ahead.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: HQL: Dividend Can Be Sustained Through 2026
data.description:
Overview

The abrdn Life Sciences Investors Fund (HQL) is structured as a closed-end fund that aims to provide attractive total returns through a portfolio of healthcare securities. What differentiates HQL from standard ETFs is the specific focus on income generation. When I previously covered HQL, I issued a buy rating due to the attractive valuation at the time. Since then, the fund has produced total returns that exceed the S&P 500. Since my last coverage, the fund has released an updated annual report for 2025, which prompted me to revisit the fund's overall value proposition for 2026.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that HQL's share price has run up nearly 22.6%. The fund continues to maintain its positive momentum. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 39.2% over the same time frame. HQL now offers investors a starting dividend yield of about 11.6%. After reviewing the latest annual report, I believe the fund will be able to sustain its high rate of payouts over the next twelve months. The fund has consistently demonstrated its ability to generate earnings that outpace distributions.

Although I believe that HQL is positioned to continue providing attractive returns, there are some weaknesses that can be improved. For instance, the latest annual report indicates that the fund is entirely reliant on net realized gains to support NAV growth and distributions. While this is great during bull markets, it can also be a detriment to the fund's success during market declines. The fund needs to significantly improve the fund's net investment income to help reduce price volatility through different market cycles.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, HQL has total assets under management of $572M that are spread across a diverse range of healthcare securities. The fund has a listed expense ratio of 1.36%, but this is misleading because it includes fees related to operating costs. When excluding these other costs, the actual management fee sits at 1.04% of total assets. Oddly, the fund's primary objective is to seek long-term capital appreciation, but the emphasis is clearly on income generation.

Additionally, the fund's name implies that HQL is most heavily allocated to Life Sciences businesses, but the breakdown by sub-sector actually reveals otherwise. In fact, HQL is most heavily allocated to Biotechnology holdings at 82.7% of assets. This is followed by Pharmaceuticals and Life Science investments, which account for 7.8% and 6.1% of assets, respectively. Therefore, HQL should actually be considered a bio-focused fund. Additionally, 88% of the fund's assets are allocated towards equity investments. ~10% of the fund's assets are allocated towards venture capital investments.

When looking at the top holdings of HQL, we can see many notable names. The fund's top ten positions make up ~28% of total assets and are diversified across all market capitalizations. The largest holding is Amgen (AMGN) at 3.92% of assets, which I recently covered and issued a buy rating on. This is followed by an allocation to many other high quality companies, including Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (REG), Insmed (INSM), uniQure (QURE), and Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX), just to name a few.

Although the fund doesn't utilize any leverage directly within its portfolio strategy, the fund can demonstrate some sensitivity to interest rates. The pharma and biotech industries can be heavily reliant on debt as a way to fuel research and development, product expansion, and operating costs. So a higher interest rate environment may delay growth initiates that can expand earnings for these businesses. Despite the Fed initiating three separate 25 basis point cuts to interest rates in 2025, the federal funds rate still remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. While the assumption is that interest rates will continue to trend downward over 2026, investors should still remain cautious about the potential for slower growth if rates remain elevated.

Updated Valuation & Performance Insights

Since HQL is structured as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of its underlying assets. Despite the recent price increase, HQL still trades at an attractive discount to NAV of 9.58%. For reference, HQL has traded at an average discount to NAV of 9.35% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that HQL's current valuation still sits at the more attractive end of its historical ten-year range. This can be an indication that it remains a great time for long-term investors to accumulate shares at these levels.

Since my last coverage, HQL has released an updated annual report for 2025. This is the most updated report that provides transparency into the fund's earnings. Net investment income amounted to a loss of $0.12 per share, while net realized gains landed at $2.91 per share. Looking back on prior years, the fund has consistently experienced negative net investment income. This can be an indication that the fund's holdings are not generating sufficient income or that the operating expenses are high at this time. Since net investment income has consistently amounted to losses, this means that HQL is entirely reliant on net realized gains in order to support the distributions and NAV growth.

The challenge is that net realized gains can wildly vary since they are based on the underlying momentum of the sector and the growth of HQL's specific holdings. This means that HQL is dependent on positive market momentum so that it can sell securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased for. Not only does this mean that HQL offers very little resilience to declining markets, but it also means that NAV growth can be hard to estimate over time. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $21.22 per share
  • 2022: $15.49 per share
  • 2023: $15.00 per share
  • 2024: $16.38 per share
  • 2025: $17.35 per share

The challenge is that if HQL relies on net realized gains to fuel growth and support distributions, the fund has to actively sell off the upside gains of its positions. So during strong market rallies, HQL's positions aren't able to grow uninterrupted, which ultimately limits capital appreciation over time. For instance, we can see below how HQL's share price has declined by nearly 9.6% over the last ten years. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to nearly 132% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that nearly all of their returns will come from the fund's ability to maintain a high rate of distributions.

Therefore, the focus on income generation means that HQL is likely to underperform more standard healthcare ETFs such as the State Street Health Care Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLV). This is the perfect demonstration that a high yield doesn't automatically translate to a higher total return over time. If you're an investor looking to maximize total returns from the healthcare sector, a more traditional growth ETF may be the best choice.

Additionally, the fund's reliance on net realized gains means that HQL may find itself in a scenario where it is paying out distributions without having earned the earnings to support them. If markets decline, this can result in NAV erosion over time as the fund continues to pay out more than it earned. For instance, XLV only saw a total loss of 2.1% throughout 2022's market decline. Meanwhile, HQL experienced a total loss of 19.2%, despite offering a higher dividend yield.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.50 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11.6%. If we take this quarterly payout and multiply it by four quarters, we get an estimated annual distribution of $2.00 per share. Referring back to the same annual report for 2025, we can see that the fund frequently generates earnings that outpace the distributions being paid out to shareholders. For this reason, I believe that HQL's payouts are likely to remain consistent over the next twelve months. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts:

  • 2021: $2.66 per share
  • 2022: ($4.26) per share
  • 2023: $0.79 per share
  • 2024: $3.04 per share
  • 2025: $2.79 per share

As we can see, HQL only struggled to generate the necessary earnings that can support distributions through 2022 and 2023. Given the circumstances at the time, such as the market decline, uncertainty related to the pandemic, and interest rate shifts, the losses are understandable. Going forward, I believe that the fund's performance may improve as the shape of the healthcare sector improves. As long as the fund continues to generate earnings that outpace distributions, the underlying NAV and share price should continue its positive momentum.

Lastly, investors need to consider the risk that the distributions received do not offer any tax efficiency for investors. For instance, the latest section 19(a) notice indicates that 100% of the distributions were supported by net realized short-term capital gains. These distributions are typically classified as ordinary dividends and have the greatest tax burden for investors. Therefore, a position in HQL would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on HQL at this time. The fund continues to trade at an attractive price-to-NAV valuation, and the latest annual report indicates that earnings still outpace the distributions being paid. Therefore, HQL is a great option for investors seeking a high rate and income stability over time. Additionally, the underlying NAV has increased over the last year, which should translate to the fund's share price rising as well. If interest rates continue to trend lower, this would improve the macroenvironment for biotech and pharma companies, which can also improve HQL's performance over the next twelve months. While there are some risks, such as underperformance against standard growth ETFs and NAV erosion during market declines, I believe that HQL's long-term performance is an indication of how efficient the fund's strategy is.

data.title: Seritage Growth Properties: Term Loan Debt Reduced After Aventura Sale
data.description:
Seritage Growth Properties (SRG)(SRG.PR.A) is inching closer to wrapping up its operations. It closed on the sale of its Aventura property in late November and made $150 million in term loan prepayments by early December. This helps reduce Seritage's interest costs to around $3.5 million per year, and Seritage's cash burn should be under $10 million per quarter now.

As the asset sales progress, that also narrows the range of potential outcomes for Seritage. I continue to estimate Seritage's value (for mid-to-late 2027) at approximately $4.00 per share and consider it likely that its value will end up in a range of around $3.50 to $4.50 per share. Thus, I am now slightly positive on Seritage at its current share price.

Term Loan Prepayments

Seritage made a $130 million term loan prepayment in late November 2025, followed by a $20 million term loan prepayment in early December 2025. These prepayments were funded by the proceeds from Seritage's recent property sales, including its Aventura sale (which closed in late November 2025).

This reduces its term loan balance to $50 million and its annual term loan interest costs to $3.5 million, which is a reduction of $10.5 million per year.

It appears that Seritage received $122 million in net proceeds for its Aventura sale, net of liabilities paid such as accrued real estate taxes and accrued tenant improvements.

Remaining Costs

Seritage's interest costs are now relatively minor and are less than the $4.9 million per year in preferred dividends that it is still paying out.

Seritage's largest use of cash is for G&A costs now. It reported $4.9 million in G&A expense in Q3 2025, which is a bit under $20 million on an annual basis.

Seritage also used $3.7 million in cash in Q3 2025 for the development of real estate, although that number should go down as Seritage sells off its remaining properties.

Meanwhile, Seritage's rental income basically covers its property operating expenses and real estate taxes with a small amount left over. I can see its cash burn coming in at under $10 million per quarter going forward now. This would be further reduced with the closing of additional asset sale transactions.

Compensation Arrangements

In November 2025, Seritage revised the retention and compensation structure for COO Eric Dinenberg. It established a six-month retention period beginning on March 16, 2026, and ending on Sept. 15, 2026. This retention period was in lieu of the automatic one-year renewal period that would have gone into effect otherwise.

If Seritage enters into a definitive agreement involving a change in the control or ownership of the company or sells substantially all its assets, then Dinenberg is entitled to a bonus of $1 million. This also requires the transactions to close by September 2027.

Meanwhile, Chief Legal Officer Matthew Fernand's employment agreement was extended by one year to March 2027.

These agreements could be extended further in the future but do seem to indicate that the general aim is to enter into sales agreements for the remaining properties during 2026, with the latest closing dates from those sales occurring in 2027.

Debt and Valuation

The paydown of $150 million of Seritage's remaining term loan debt is a positive, although one that I had already assumed would happen by the end of 2025.

There is now $50 million in term loan debt and $70 million in preferred shares (at liquidation preference) ranking ahead of the common shares. Seritage may also continue to hold approximately $60 million in cash and restricted cash.

My baseline assumption is that Seritage will close its final deals by mid-to-late 2027 and that it will have approximately $225 million in net cash after paying off its term loan debt and redeeming its preferred shares. This also assumes around $50 million in cash burn for G&A, preferred dividends, cash interest, and development costs between the end of 2025 and mid-to-late 2027.

This translates into an estimated value of around $4.00 per share for Seritage at that point. I would consider a range of around $3.50 to $4.50 per share to be reasonable for Seritage. The main unknowns are how long it will take Seritage to complete all its asset sales and what net proceeds it will receive relative to the estimated range it provided around gross proceeds.

The following table shows my estimated breakdown of cash expenses by period.

The proceeds from Seritage's asset sales going forward should be compared to their prior balance sheet values. I believe they will track closely to those balance sheet values.

Conclusion

Seritage now has only $50 million in remaining term loan debt, which results in relatively minimal interest costs. Seritage's quarterly cash burn should be under $10 million now as well.

My midpoint estimate of Seritage's value is $4.00 per share, and I believe that it is most likely that its value will end up in the $3.50 to $4.50 per share range. This is based on its remaining asset sales closing by mid-to-late 2027 and it having around $50 million in cash burn from the start of 2026 until then.

Seritage is now trading slightly lower than that range, and I am now modestly positive on its valuation. I believe that covered calls could be a reasonable strategy too, although Seritage's option volumes appear to be relatively limited.

data.title: Avita Medical: Set Up For A Turnaround
data.description:
In 2025, the business of Avita Medical (RCEL) was badly damaged by unforeseen payment problems affecting its customers by the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS). With those issues in the rearview mirror, a turnaround in both its business and share price seems likely in 2026.

Background

Avita Medical Inc. has had a challenging year in 2025. So challenging that it made the move to replace its CEO on October 16th, 2025, with its Board Chairman, Cary Vance, now serving as the new interim CEO. Multiple issues lead to this change. Consistently failing to adequately forecast revenue growth was the major issue. This led to bumping up against debt covenants and the overstaffing of its sales force that eventually had to be dialed back. However, the biggest negative factor was an unexpected screw-up beyond the direct control of management. Starting on January 1st, 2025, changes made by the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid (CMS) resulted in the failure to promptly pay Avita Medical's customers for using the company's innovative anchor product, RECELL Go, for burn and other acute wound injuries. The company was late in understanding this major impediment to its business and did not alert shareholders until a press release on August 7, 2025. It doesn't take a rocket scientist to understand that if a surgeon or a hospital does not get paid for using a company's product, it really doesn't matter how innovative and beneficial the product is to patients or economically. As a result of this huge payment mess, sales stalled, and shares have declined sharply over the last year:

Background

This is my sixth positive article on Avita Medical. The initial article was written in February 2019; the most recent was in December 2023. After a spectacular run following the initial article, the pandemic and failures of the company to execute on its own projections have plagued the share price even before the CMS payment issues. Some might ask, why would you write another article given the company's consistent failures? The answer is a continued belief in the company's innovative, high-margin products. With the CMS payment issues on the way to full resolution, the timing now seems right for an update in the belief that the shares have bottomed out and the company is finally positioned to meet its full potential.

The Avita Medical Acute Care Wound Platform

The backbone product that differentiates the company in the wound care market is RECELL GO. This is a truly remarkable regenerative medicine innovation that uses a small sliver of a patient's skin to seed a wound bed 80 times larger than the sample taken. It's a relatively quick and simple process for the burn or trauma surgeon compared to the standard of care. The small skin sample is placed into a RECELL GO cartridge along with a buffer and a proprietary enzyme. The cartridge is then placed into the RECELL Go Processing Device that, with the press of a button, automatically disaggregates the skin sample into a spray mixture of regenerative skin cell components. These cells act as the cellular seeds that allow a large wound to regenerate new skin evenly and relatively quickly:

The main advantages of the RECELL system over the standard of care are the timeline to patient healing and superior cosmetic results that include pigmentation. RECELL Go Mini was recently added to the product line for use in smaller wounds.

From a business standpoint, the speed of recovery provides a substantial monetary benefit to the burn and trauma centers that are paid a lump sum based on the type and extent of the injury. Under this payment system, treatment time is money, as illustrated in the company's presentation below:

As shown in the analysis above, the RECELL system saves approximately 5.6 days, or $42,377 in costs, per patient stay. This provides a strong monetary incentive for hospitals to use the system, making it an anchor product that should sell itself.

Vertical Integration in Wound Care

While RECELL Go is Avita's anchor product, the company has recently taken the path to vertically integrate its product line, a strategy that will provide sales synergies. A few years ago, the company considered pursuing private pay cosmetic rejuvenation procedures and a treatment for vitiligo, but the company made a smart move (in my opinion) to instead focus on other complementary aspects of acute wound care. Within the last couple of years, Avita literally became vertically integrated within the wound bed itself with two high-margin, generously priced additions to its product line called PermeaDerm and Cohealyx. The following chart shows the revenue opportunity of each product and how they are used in acute injury:

All three Avita wound healing products have been shown to work faster than competing products and are backed by both clinical and preclinical data. Avita recently executed exclusive licensing agreements for both PermeaDerm and Cohealyx, but they haven't yet significantly impacted the revenue stream so far. To get these products into surgeons' hands, they must gain approval from hospital Value Analysis Committees (VACs), and this step has gone slower than expected, as it did when Avita first launched RECELL. In a very recent update, the company reported the first use of all three wound healing products by a surgeon in a single wound bed at the 2026 Boswick Burn & Wound Symposium being held January 24th-29th.

Avita recently signed exclusive distribution agreements that brought these complementary products into Avita's product lineup. For PermeaDerm, Avita retains 60% of the average sales price and pays 40% to Stedical Scientific, net of Avita's manufacturing costs. For Cohealyx, the initial term of the exclusive development and distribution agreement with Regenity Biosciences is five years, with an automatic extension of an additional five years, contingent upon meeting certain criteria. According to the initial press release, revenue sharing from sales of Cohealyx is expected to be equal to 50% for the first two years and then increase to 60%. Cohealyx is manufactured by Regency.

The Critical CMS Payment Issue Update

As previously mentioned, on August 7th, 2025, Aldeyra disclosed the CMS payment issue for the first time in this excerpt:

In November 2024, the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) announced new Category I CPT codes for the use of RECELL as part of its 2025 rule update. Unlike the standard process, CMS did not assign a national payment rate. Instead, it assigned pricing responsibility to the Medicare Administrative Contractors (MACS) under a process known as contractor pricing, an approach CMS occasionally uses when initiating long-term code changes.

Following the implementation of the new codes, claims for the use of RECELL were submitted starting in January 2025. Under the contractor pricing model, it is the MACs' responsibility to adjudicate claims by either assigning a payment rate and reimbursing the claim, or denying the claim, which would allow the provider to appeal and trigger an adjudication through that process. However, in this instance, MACs neither assigned a price or assigned an inadequate price and failed to adjudicate claims in a timely manner. As a result, claims accumulated from January through June, creating a significant backlog of unpaid claims and inadequately paid claims to providers for RECELL procedures. This lack of resolution created uncertainty among providers regarding payment expectations and timelines, which led to a reduction in RECELL utilization during the first half of the year.

While AVITA continued receiving payment for RECELL, this provider reimbursement issue constrained demand, meaningfully impacting revenue. For example, across AVITA’s top ten hospital accounts, RECELL revenue declined by approximately $5 million when comparing the second half of 2024 to the first half of 2025. The Company estimates that overall demand for RECELL declined by approximately 20%, with revenue declining by approximately $10 million during this period.

The company provided an update on this crucially important matter in a presentation at the 44th J.P. Morgan 44th Annual Healthcare Conference. Avita has made progress in finally cleaning up this issue. The chart below (from the conference) indicated that 4 of the 7 MACS have published codes as of the conference, and the new CEO stated that he expected the remaining 3 MACS to publish by the end of January. On January 26th, I checked the three remaining MAC websites and found payment codes now published for two. I was unable to find the RECELL payment codes (15011-15018) at National Government Services, and it is critical that this last MAC corrects this situation.

Looking for a Revenue Rebound

The premise of this article is that the clear benefits of RECELL Go will lead to more rapid adoption now that the CMS payment problems, a major setback to growth, have been substantially resolved. With the recent rollout of attractively priced PermeaDerm and Cohealyx products, the company is positioned to experience piggyback growth in complementary products that are sold to the same customers by the same salesforce. Once they get past the formidable step of VAC approval, this should be a very efficient selling setup to grow revenues with relatively limited expense growth. The new CEO is squarely focused on execution, and his ability to deliver is the wild card here. There are no more excuses.

The following is a look back at the last five years of commercial sales growth for perspective:

Even with the major CMS payment impediment, 2025 commercial revenues grew by 11%. RECELL products have a healthy gross margin, 83.6% in the most recent 3rd quarter, so a turnaround in revenues will go a long way in closing the gap in the negative cash flow the company has experienced since inception. Management is projecting a revenue growth rate of only 15% in 2026. I suspect the new CEO is being conservative given the company's history of badly missing estimates over the last couple of years. The hope is for upside surprises in 2026 that can drive shareholder value.

Balance Sheet and Recent Debt Restructuring

The regular quarterly revenue misses in 2025 created debt covenant problems throughout 2025. On January 13th, 2026, Avita took a big step in mitigating its near term balance sheet risk and the fear of a secondary offering when it refinanced its existing OrbiMed credit agreement:

Today, AVITA Medical also announced the closing of a five-year credit facility providing up to $60 million in capital from Perceptive Advisors, a healthcare-focused investment firm. An initial $50 million has been funded with the option to draw an additional $10 million through the end of the first quarter 2027. The proceeds of the transaction will be used to repay the Company’s outstanding debt and support the further growth of its acute wound care portfolio.

As part of the new credit facility, the Company established trailing twelve-month (TTM) revenue covenants aligned with AVITA Medical’s current operating trajectory. As set forth in the Credit Agreement, the initial TTM revenue covenant is $68.5 million for the first quarter ending March 31, 2026, and $73 million for the full year 2026. Accordingly, based on the Company’s reported financial results for the three quarters ended December 31, 2025, the Company will be required to achieve $15.4 million in revenue in the first quarter 2026.

Perceptive Advisors had this to say about the new credit agreement in the press release:

“AVITA Medical has established a differentiated position in acute wound care with a clear path to value creation through both commercial execution and clinical advancement,” said Sam Chawla, Portfolio Manager at Perceptive Advisors. “With multiple near-term catalysts, we believe the Company is well positioned to scale efficiently and progress toward sustainable profitability, and we are pleased to support the management team through this next phase of growth.”

In the near term, this transaction removes the immediate threat of a dilutive secondary that has likely weighed on shares throughout the second half of 2025 and early January. Assuming Avita can meet the much lower quarterly revenue levels as specified above, they should; the company now has an additional $10,000,000 to draw upon as it strives toward cash flow breakeven.

As of September 30, 2025, the company held cash and marketable securities totaling $23,300,000 and had a cash burn of $6,200,000 for that quarter, a significant reduction from the $10,100,000 cash burn a year earlier as expense reductions kicked in. The new $50,000,000 loan proceeds paid off the existing credit facility that had a balance of $42,449,000 on September 30th. This difference ($7,501,000) should more than cover the cash burn in the fourth quarter of 2025. Aside from the revenue covenants, the new agreement requires Avita to maintain a minimum cash balance of $5,000,000.

Bottom Line

Avita Medical is back to where it should have been at the beginning of 2025, at the precipice of an exciting year of sales growth. In January 2025, Avita projected full-year 2025 commercial revenue in the range of $100 to $106 million, or 55% to 65% growth over 2024. The CMS payment issues played a large role in missing that guidance by almost $30,000,000! For 2026, Avita has tempered down revenue expectations to about $82.5 million, only a 15% growth rate. It would be natural for new management to be conservative given the history of overpromising and underdelivering. In my view, a 15% growth rate would be a disappointment.

Avita lost an entire year of sales momentum when CMS unexpectedly pulled the rug from under it. The company was at fault for not recognizing this huge problem much sooner. That is water under the bridge right now. Could there be hidden icebergs ahead? Of course. However, now that the CMS iceberg is just about cleared up, it seems likely we could see a significant turnaround ahead. The market often rewards high growth companies with interesting, innovative products, and sentiment can turn on a dime.

Avita Medical is trading at a market cap of only $134,000,000 and has $50,000,000 in debt from its new credit agreement. Shares seem cheap for a growth company with projected 2026 revenues of $82,500,000 and very favorable gross margins. If things go well, Avita could be a big winner in 2026.

Focus on Risks

Avita Medical is a microcap company and has $50,000,000 in long term debt from its new credit facility. The company has never been cash flow positive. The obvious primary risk here is that they fail to grow revenues and miss the lowered debt covenant revenue targets. Eventually, like any company, they will need to be positive cash flow to remain a going concern.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Top 2026 Long Idea investment competition, which runs through February 28. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: IonQ: Critical Department Of War Memo
data.description:
Investment Thesis

IonQ (IONQ) has been on a tear since I last wrote about the quantum computing giant, with shares up 5.3% YTD (beating the market) and shares up almost 291% over the last 5 years.

Since I last wrote on them, shares are up 76.77%.

I believe the market is starting to figure out how powerful IonQ’s technology is with the company making a set of key hires, and launching their division in Italy, IonQ Italia.

In the US the company is hitting its stride. But one memo from the Department of War could pose a huge risk to their defense business.

Late last year, the Department of War signed a memo banning the purchase of Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) encryption devices. This is a huge setback for all quantum companies (including IonQ). There are arguments that Quantum Key Distribution based encryption methods are mathematically unhackable.

The Department of War has banned the purchase of encryption systems that use their technology, effective immediately.

On the surface this sounds like a huge risk to a quantum computing company whose largest customer is the US government.

However, I actually think this memo will clear the way for IonQ to do well. Quantum Key Distribution is a key part of other quantum defense offerings.

For IonQ? It's a small part of the story. The company has multiple other business lines that will allow them to excel in their government contracting business while laying the groundwork for them to later revoke/remove this memo with key hires.

In essence, this memo will likely be damaging for other quantum companies that aim to work with the Department of War.

For IonQ? It’s a case of the last man standing.

With this, shares actually remain a strong buy despite the short term headwinds.

Why I’m Doing Follow Up Coverage

IonQ has been on a tear operationally since I last wrote on the quantum computing giant last year. Just this week, the firm announced that they would be acquiring semiconductor firm SkyWater in a $1.8 billion cash-stock deal that helps IonQ add more key revenue streams to their income statement while also providing another key pipeline for management to overlay quantum opportunities onto.

In essence, the company is off to the races in 2026. This doesn’t mean, however, things have gone perfectly to plan.

Cryptologists working for the Department of War have flagged concerns around quantum key distribution, a method of handling encryption keys for systems that are trying to remove cybersecurity vulnerabilities from future attacks from quantum computers.

In essence, quantum computers via QKD offer a potentially mathematically unhackable method to transmit information. The Department of War has flagged and banned this method as part of their procurement process effective immediately.

On the surface, this feels like a huge setback. Underneath, IonQ is already two steps ahead. The reason I am doing this follow up coverage is to show how IonQ’s management is extremely proactive to changing regulatory dynamics.

Bad News From The Department Of War

In late November, the United States Department of War (DOW) came out with what I believe is a controversial memo. This memo outlines the Department of War’s stance on burgeoning cryptography standards used to help safeguard military personnel and military equipment.

I highly encourage you to read it.

In this memo, signed by outgoing acting DOW CIO Katherine Arrington, the Department of War bans the purchase of quantum key distribution technology effective immediately.

The full language:

DoW Components will not test, evaluate, pilot, use, or procure the following technologies…Quantum Confidentiality or Keying Technologies. Examples of these include but are not limited to quantum key distribution...

For IonQ, this is a huge setback. Quantum key distribution is how quantum companies (including IonQ) are meshing their entire quantum infrastructure stack together. The Department of War is one of IonQ’s largest customers. Many ally countries (including in Europe) are leaning into the use of QKD technology.

This means that any quantum computing technology purchased by the Department of War will need to use one of the 3 NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) PQC (Post Quantum Cryptography) approved algorithms to communicate the data these quantum computers generate.

The issue with these NIST approved algorithms? They’re not battle-tested yet because quantum computers capable of testing these algorithms have not been invented yet. In fact, NIST originally had 4 algorithm candidates that were supposed to protect against post-quantum computers.

The issue? One of them was almost immediately cracked as soon as it was made public. Who's to say the other 3 algorithms will stand the test of time?

Quantum key distribution based cybersecurity is unhackable because it is based on core principles in physics.

What IonQ Says

I have not found a ton of research online about this memo, so on the surface, it's hard to tell what the true quantifiable impact will be. But, to be clear, there will be an impact. IonQ (along with many other publicly traded quantum companies in the US) has large SBIR (small business innovation research) contracts with the Department of War. Some of these contracts explicitly involve research around quantum key distribution, so these terms will need to be modified.

The question is how much will this cost IonQ in future revenue? I’m not sure. Here’s what I do know.

Last month, as I was doing research on this article, I reached out to investor relations at IonQ about this DOW memo. They provided the following statement:

As a matter of policy, we don't comment on internal directives the DOW is issuing.

In essence, IonQ is aware of the memo (as they should be as a government contractor), but they have chosen to not comment.

Here’s why that matters.

Part of the way 8K disclosures work for publicly traded companies in the US is that they have something called a “duty to correct.”

That is, if the company provided information before that now appears to be incorrect or misleading, they usually have to correct it. This can typically be done intra-quarter by filing an 8-K, which is a form with the SEC.

At the time of this writing, IonQ has not filed any 8Ks related to this change at the Department of War. Given how late the next earnings call is (it is not projected to happen until February 26th), the company does not really have any argument that they can save any material information related to change in guidance until the earnings call.

Importantly, last quarter the company raised its FY 2025 guidance to project revenues of $106 million to $110 million. If this memo was going to impact the timing of any milestone payments from the Department of War that were material to their Q4 revenue, the duty to correct doctrine would have provided that IonQ update the general public on this risk to their business.

In essence, IonQ has not filed any notices with the SEC that this memo will materially affect their business. I believe that this memo is a big enough deal that, if it was going to impact their business, they would have had to file a notice by this point.

That doesn’t mean the memo can’t slow growth (there are arguments it will)...

What it does likely mean is that IonQ does not think the slowing growth (from QKD product lines to the DOW) will be overall damaging to their trajectory.

On top of this, IonQ has two aces up their sleeve.

Why I Am Still Bullish

IonQ is the largest publicly traded pure-play quantum company in the world. Thankfully, IonQ has been able to leverage their stance to offset this DOW memo that affects their QKD offering. IonQ was able to do this in two parts.

First, and whether this was through sheer luck or brute force will, IonQ was able to convince the acting CIO (Chief Information Officer) of the DOW to join the company after she left the Department of War in December.

That’s right, the same person that signed off on this memo effectively banning QKD technology from the Department of War now works for IonQ.

She will now work entirely to help IonQ develop and sell their products (in part) into the DOW. I expect her to also have a change of opinion now on whether QKD technology is good enough to safeguard America’s military.

Just this past week, former CIO Katherine Arrington (now CIO of IonQ) posted a great review of her first week on the job at IonQ. I will link the LinkedIn post here for you to watch yourself.

I would say she is likely no-longer a quantum key distribution skeptic.

The second major key move was IonQ’s acquisition of SkyWater. SkyWater is key because they are a leading lab for manufacturing quantum semiconductor components along with chip packaging. IonQ is showing the Department of War (and the US government as a whole) with this acquisition that they are the most serious in this rapidly burgeoning space at controlling their key supply chain.

This one-two punch style from IonQ likely does not stem just from one memo. But it does show how the company has a comprehensive strategy to fight off changing regulatory winds in an industry that is changing at an accelerating pace.

Valuation

While the DOW memo will likely be an overhang for a little bit, I believe the company actually deserves to trade at a higher valuation than where they are today.

The recent acquisition of SkyWater is a big catalyst for this.

If we look at the current forward revenue estimates (exclusive of SkyWater), we get an average estimate of $192.63MM. Without the acquisition, this would represent 77.37% YoY top line growth.

When we add in the SkyWater acquisition, based on their 2026 revenue projections, we reach an estimated revenue run rate of $609.13MM.

All together, IonQ will now go from a roughly $100MM run rate to start this year to an $800MM run rate by the beginning of 2027. With this, the price to sales ratio should come down.

Currently, the forward price to sales is 141.41. After this, we could see a price to sales ratio closer to 23.

I think the price-to-sales ratio should be closer to 30 given their undeniable, industry leading position.

If we saw IonQ reach this forward price-to-sales ratio based on the new revenue run rate, this would represent a 30.4% upside from here.

Bottom Line

While the memo from the Department of War certainly feels like a setback for IonQ and the entire quantum computing industry, I am not fazed as a shareholder. IonQ’s business is far more than the QKD technology that now faces hurdles to deployment inside the US military branch. I believe there are more than enough commercial (and international defense) deployments to prove that their system works.

IonQ continues to be a leader in quantum computing and is showing Wall Street that scalable quantum computing technology sales are possible. From protecting international finance, to developing technology that will be used to help speed up drug discoveries, I’m confident the company can survive this memo.

With that being said, the memo warrants caution to anyone who's an investor (including myself). I would not be surprised if shares of IonQ (or other quantum computing companies) sell off in the aftermath of this. I think these remain buying opportunities.

With this, shares are still a strong buy.

data.title: Rekor Systems: Little-Known Stock Near Inflection Point
data.description:
2026 is proving to be a tricky market to invest in, though many of us were prepared for volatility with the major indices exiting 2025 at heightened multiples. To maintain gains this year, I continue to advocate for a rotation of our portfolios into small- and mid-cap stocks to reduce our exposure to a possible downward multiples correction in large-cap momentum names.

Often, these small-cap stocks are rebound stories, but here, investors are too focused on the risks and not on the cheap valuations that price in these risks. Rekor Systems (REKR) is a good example of this. The roadway intelligence software company has seen its share price decline ~25% over the past year, with the stock also down more than 50% relative to peaks above $3 briefly notched in October.

I last wrote a "Buy" rating on Rekor in September, when the stock was trading near $1.80. While I fully acknowledge that this is a position that has required tremendous patience, I also believe that Rekor's upcoming earnings and the fact that the company is beginning to convert large statewide deals into revenue will be a fantastic catalyst for the stock in 2026. I retain my "Buy" rating here.

The next major catalyst for Rekor will be its Q4 earnings print (expected in mid- to late February), where the company should also release its expectations for FY26. With expectations already low for the company (Wall Street is only expecting ~21% revenue growth, which is slower than growth in the most recent quarter and may not fully reflect all the company's latest deals), this is a good "Buy" before the earnings cycle.

Revenue Begins Accelerating, With the Georgia Deal a Major Catalyst

Let's first turn our attention to the recent progress that Rekor has been making. As a reminder for investors who are newer to the stock, the company is mainly a supplier to government (state-level) transportation agencies, giving highway authorities the software to monitor roadway speeds and identify license plates, alongside analyzing traffic patterns and congestion. Needless to say, Rekor relies on a few anchor customers to bring in revenue, and its deals take time to convert into revenue.

Q3 reflected the first indications of Rekor's turnaround. In Q3, the company's revenue of $14.2 million grew 35% y/y, reversing past a 1% y/y decline in Q2 and a 5% y/y decline in Q1.

The revenue bump reflects, in part, the materialization of the company's deal signed in Texas, which kicked off in June. As a reminder, the company didn't provide exact specifics for the monetization of its Texas deal, but it did cite an eight-figure win spanning multiple years. We're encouraged to see the deal momentum turn into actual revenue progress in Q3, especially as revenue substantially beat Wall Street's expectations of $12.9 million (+23% y/y) in the quarter by a 12-point margin.

But the best news is that new major deals are ahead. The Texas deal now stands in second place behind the company's newly announced statewide deal with the Georgia Department of Transportation. As shown in the chart below, the company has been more specific on the specifics of this deal: Georgia is expected to deliver a minimum of $50 million in revenue over an 8-year period. The deal has the potential to increase to $100 million in total value.

Again, this is just one state and one deal. Currently, consensus is only expecting Rekor to generate $61 million in revenue in FY26, which is 21% y/y above FY25 revenue of $50 million. On a straight-line basis (though revenue ramp will likely be back-end loaded), assuming $50 million in Georgia revenue over eight years means $6.25 million in incremental annual revenue, which, in itself, is already 13% of the company's FY25 revenue. That's not to mention the additional ramping of the eight-figure deal in Texas (we now assume that "eight figures" means more than $10 million but less than $50 million, given the company now cites Georgia as its largest win), where the company has cited immense potential to expand beyond the Austin/Central Texas area once the pilots are complete.

On a smaller note, Rekor also signed a $1 million initial win with South Carolina, and management notes that the forward pipeline is healthy. Per CFO Joseph Nalepa's remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

We continue to see strong momentum in our sales pipeline and active deployments, giving us confidence in our ability to continue to drive growth going forward.

Profit Inflection as Software Contracts Mature

The other stock catalyst that we have to be aware of is that as Rekor's major contracts begin to turn into reliable recurring software revenue, the company's margin profile is also elevating.

As shown in the chart above, adjusted gross margins rose nearly 20 points year-over-year to 63.4%. Rekor had long had a margin deficit to most SaaS peers because much of its business was new, and low-margin services and implementation work weighed down its profitability. As deals like Texas and Georgia move past the implementation phase and generate recurring revenue, we see Rekor continuing to stabilize its margins closer to most software peers in the ~70% range.

Meanwhile, adjusted EBITDA losses reduced by 84% to just -$1.4 million, and this is a trend we expect to continue to see in Q4 and beyond.

Valuation, Q4 Look-ahead, and Key Takeaways

The best appeal to investing in Rekor, meanwhile, is a cheap share price. At current share prices near $1.30, Rekor trades at a $171.9 million market cap. Netting off the $4.1 million of cash and deposits on Rekor's balance sheet against $14.9 million of debt gives us an enterprise value of $182.7 million. This sits at a 3.0x EV/FY26 revenue multiple against consensus' $60.9 million (+21% y/y) expectations for FY26.

In my view, the stock is well-poised to rebound with the upcoming Q4 earnings cycle unlocking several potential catalysts. In my view, the core items that we need to watch for are:

  • Continued revenue maturity from recent major contracts. We expect more monetization from the Texas and Georgia deals to help keep Rekor firmly in double-digit growth territory.
  • Pipeline conversion. Management has noted a healthy sales pipeline, and we're hoping to see a few more major deals announced that can help to lift revenue expectations for FY26.
  • Profit inflection in FY26. Rekor already came close to breakeven adjusted EBITDA in Q3. We're hopeful that the company releases an FY26 outlook that calls for positive adjusted EBITDA, which would allow us to justify Rekor's valuation from a bottom-line perspective for the first time.

All in all, this is a great lesser-known stock to bank on that is full of catalysts to drive a rebound. "Buy" here ahead of earnings.

data.title: EMLC: High Risks From Capital Erosion And Currency Rates
data.description:
Strategy

VanEck J.P. Morgan EM Local Currency Bond ETF (EMLC) was launched on 7/22/2010 and tracks the J.P. Morgan GBI-EM Global Core Index. EMLC has a portfolio of 487 debt securities, a 30-day SEC yield of 5.86%, a 12-month trailing yield of 5.79%, a yield-to-worst of 6.06%, and an expense ratio of 0.30%. Distributions are paid monthly.

As described in the prospectus by VanEck, the underlying index is comprised of bonds issued by emerging market governments in the local currency of the issuer. The index may also include bonds of supranational agencies. Unlike dollar-denominated bonds, distributions and face value in USD of the constituents may vary with currency rates. The portfolio turnover rate was 25% in the most recent fiscal year.

Portfolio

The fund is almost exclusively invested in sovereign debt (93% of asset value) and is geographically well-diversified, no country weighting more than 9%. The largest issuers in the portfolio are the governments of China (8.6%), India (7.6%), and Indonesia (7.6%). Asia is the top region with about 40% of assets, followed by Latin America (24%) and Eastern Europe (21%).

* calculated by the author from the holding list.

EMLC has a moderate credit risk in aggregate, with 78.5% of asset value in securities receiving an investment-grade rating (BBB or above).

The fund is focused on short- and mid-term maturities, with a weighted average maturity of 7.67 years. As for sensitivity to rate variations, the average duration of 5.21 years points to moderate risk.

Performance

The next table uses as a benchmark a developed market government bond index, represented by iShares International Treasury Bond ETF (IGOV). EMLC has outperformed IGOV by 1.3% annualized since 7/29/2010, with higher volatility.
