Funds focused on debt in local currencies (EMLC, EBND, LEMB) have suffered from the U.S. dollar's strength since 2013, underperforming their peers investing in dollar-denominated bonds.

Takeaway

VanEck J.P. Morgan EM Local Currency Bond ETF offers exposure to emerging market government bonds, with geographical diversification, moderate credit risk, and interest rate risk. EMLC has outperformed a developed market government bond benchmark, but shows significant erosion in value and income amplified by inflation. Additionally, the weakness of emerging market currencies has been a drag compared to dollar-denominated bond ETFs. The 6% yield is not high enough to offset these risks. For these reasons, I give EMLC a "Sell" rating from a long-term perspective. Nonetheless, this ETF may be useful as a trading or tactical allocation instrument.

data.title: Arbor Realty: Outlook Improves As Yield Curve Steepens
data.description:
Arbor Realty stock: previous thesis and new catalysts

I last analyzed Arbor Realty Trust (NYSE:ABR) stock on Dec 11 with an article titled "Arbor Realty: Why Insiders Kept Buying (Rating Upgrade)". That article focused on its latest insider activities and upgraded my rating on the stock to buy. Since then, there have been a few new catalysts evolving around this mREIT stock. Given the sensitivity of mREIT businesses on interest rates, the most important catalyst on my radar is the status of the yield curve. The recent transition of the yield curve from an extended period of inversion to a positive slope marks a definitive shift in the macroeconomic environment facing ABR in my model.

To be more explicit , the data below shows this transition in my details. Following the Federal Reserve's December rate cut, the spread between short term rates (taken as the 3-month treasury rates) and long-term treasury rates (taken as the 10-year treasury rates) have finally become positive. Moreover, the spread has been on a widening trend since my last writing and currently hovers around 0.55%. As a background, the yield curve began to be inverted in mid-2022 and remained that way till earlier 2025. In most of 2025, the spread fluctuated very tightly around zero.

Next, I will explain why I expect the yield curve to further steepen in the near future and will detail my thoughts on the implications for ABR stock.

Yield curve could further steepen

The reasons behind my above expectation are quite straightforward. I expect short-term rates to further decrease while long-term rates to remain elevated. Let me start with the short-term rates. According to the following CME Group FedWatch Tool, currently, the market expects dominant odds for the Fed rates to be further lowered in 2026. As an example, in about 6 months (in the June 17 FOMC meeting), the most likely scenario the market sees is for short-term rates to decrease to the 3.25% to 3.5% range with a 46.8% probability. Also, note that the probability for this scenario has been increasing. It was 40.6% a month ago.

The Fed only controls the short end of the interest rates. Long term bonds rates are largely controlled by market forces and have their own minds. As an example, the next chart shows that 10-year treasury rates have actually increased slightly since the Dec rate cut. Looking ahead, I expect long-term rates to remain elevated given the current inflation, US debt ratings, and also the shifting monetary order (as detailed in a recent article about Ray Dalio's all-weather portfolio).

With the above expectations, I see large odds for continued steeping of the yield curve, which should be a tailwind for mREIT stocks like ABR. The detailed dynamics of mREIT's profits are explained in an earlier article. The gist is quoted below to ease reference:

… mREIT stocks in general (and ABR to a large degree) makes money on the spread between long-term and short-term rates. The thicker the long-short spread, the easier it is for this mREIT stock to make money.

Of course, the above argument applies to mREIT stocks in general and won't only benefit ABR. However, I do see a few reasons that could make a wider yield spread benefit ABR more. The first reason on my list involves ABR's particular portfolio exposure. ABR has sizable exposure to bridge and mezzanine loans and mortgage-related securities focused on multifamily, single-family rental, and commercial real estate. A wider spread could help such exposure substantially in my view due to more favorable loan originations and thicker margins from higher interest rates. The second reason on my list is ABR's use of leverage. This is more of double-edged sword and I will elaborate more later.

ABR's valuation

Despite the potential tailwind, ABR's valuation is very attractive both in absolute and relative terms. In absolute terms, the stock trades at a single-digit FWD P/E ratio of 8.2x as seen in the chart below. Furthermore, the stock price is also deeply discounted from its book value. As seen, its Price to Book ratio is only 0.64x currently.

In relative terms, a comparative valuation in the mREIT space is always tricky in my view. With the differences in their holdings, sizes, and also levels of leverage, it is difficult to find two exactly comparable mREIT stocks. Ladder Capital (LADR) is ABR's most direct comparison in my mind. Both are internally managed mREITs that focus heavily on bridge loans and transitional commercial real estate. And both share similar sizes with a market cap of around $1.6 billion for ABR and $1.4 billion for LADR. The next closet peers in my mind is Starwood Property Trust (STWD). STWD shares similar exposures but its massive size (with a market cap of around $6.7 billion, more than 4x of ABR) often makes it less of a "direct" peer to ABR.

With the above caveats in mind, you can see that ABR's valuation metrics are substantially discounted from both peers. Again, its P/B ratio of 0.64 indicates a significant 36% discount to its book value, whereas peers STWD and LADR trade much closer to their intrinsic book values with P/B ratios of 0.98 and 0.94, respectively. In terms of P/E ratios, ABR FY1 ratio of 8.2x is about 24% discounted from STWD and about 31% from LADR.

Other risks and final thoughts

To echo my earlier point of leverage, Arbor Realty's balance sheet is significantly more leveraged than its peers, which could be a double-edged sword as aforementioned. To wit, the chart below summarizes the financial leverage and debt profiles for ABR and its two peers mentioned above. As seen, ABR's current total net debt is approximately $10 billion, vs. about $2.9 billion for LADR despite their similar market caps. Arbor Realty's debt-to-equity ratio of 335% is also far above STWD's 284% and LADR's 201%. As such, if interest rates indeed materialize in the direction I anticipated above, ABR could benefit more than its peers. But if the development is in the opposite direction, ABR would be hurt more. Finally, bear in mind that the differences in their leverage ratios can also offset some of ABR's valuation discounts mentioned above.

An mREIT article won't be complete without a look at the dividend. On this front, ABR's yield is attractive either by horizontal or vertical comparison. Vertically, ABR's forward yield of 15.58% is significantly above its four-year average of 12.15% by about 28% as seen in the chart below. Horizontally, its yield leads its peer group in every displayed category too as seen. Its forward yield of 15.58% is significantly above STWD's 10.59% and LADR's 8.30%. Such a current yield offers investors substantially thicker downside proration than its peers and help to counter-balance the risks.

To close, this revisit to ABR stock is mainly triggered by the changes in the yield curve since my last writing. Given the sensitivity of mREIT stock to interest rates, I believe the recent normalization of the yield curve from an extended period of inversion is a fundamental shift for ABR's profit outlook. Furthermore, I also expect the yield curve to continue steepening in the near future. These consideration have led me to reiterate my buy rating under the current conditions.

data.title: Pure Cycle Regains Momentum From Oil And Gas And A Clearer Strategy
data.description:
Last time I spoke about Pure Cycle Corporation (PCYO), oil & gas operators were reducing water demand from PCYO’s resources due to lower oil prices. The housing sector was pretty slow with very high mortgage rates, and PCYO management once stated that it was inclined to sell its real estate assets to acquire additional water reservoirs, should an opportunity arise in the future, which, in my view, would be a mistake, as it would reduce the business's cash flow.

Well, the situation has flipped. Now, 10 to 20 wells are being drilled, some are completed, and some have already started operations. Mortgage rates are declining steadily, which may increase housing demand in Sky Ranch, and management outlined a better strategy going forward. Instead of selling its cash-generating real estate to possibly acquire water reservoirs, it changed to acquiring nearby land and possibly building rental houses, which will put its large water portfolio to use and monetize it faster.

The Company

I did a more detailed explanation about PCYO in the last article, but if you just want to know the basics. PCYO is a water utility company operating in Denver, Colorado, that also develops land and, more recently, started buying and renting houses in its planned community, Sky Ranch. Its revenues come from water usage by oil & gas companies and residential customers, from connection fees (tap fees), from land development (including lot sales and project management fees), and lastly, from housing rentals.

Last Quarter

An important source of revenue for PCYO comes from oil and gas, which has been almost absent in recent quarters due to the lower demand and a high supply situation, resulting in lower oil prices and idle wells. However, management stated in the last earnings call:

“They're building these large multi-well pad sites that will have somewhere between 10 and 20 wells on each of these pad sites. So they're really concentrating their activity to a pad site and they have the directional drilling on these pad sites."

and

“We've got a number of wells that have been drilled and completed, and then they're just starting fracking later this month, and they'll be fracking most of the year.”

I am not an oil & gas specialist, but from what I researched, these initiatives of building multiple wells in the same spot function like an assembly line in a factory, where they are able to reduce costs by cutting down “walking time”, recycling water, utilizing the same infrastructure, like pipelines and roads, for multiple wells, and having operations going 24/7, with one well being pumped while the others are being reset. This lowers the breakeven point for drillers, where, before, the breakeven price would be above $60, and now, it can be in the 50s or even lower. This means that, in the short term, PCYO will return to receiving oil & gas revenue from water usage, royalties, and maybe more Tap fees. If you look at the past, in some quarters, oil & gas were the second largest revenue contributor for PCYO. In 2024, it accounted for about half of the company’s total revenue when removing land sales.

In the housing segment, the company is set for improved demand with the help of declining mortgage rates. There are now 7 homebuilders in Sky Ranch, where before there were only 4. As Sky Ranch development phases advance, PCYO also receives its contracted houses. Management mentioned that it now has 19 homes completed and all rented, which is a great indication of demand. An additional 40 contracted homes are expected in 2026, of which they expect 4 to 5 to be finished each month, which is in line with previous estimates of having about 60 homes for rent at the end of 2026. As Sky Ranch continues to grow, it will reach a point where it will be attractive for commercial development, which is an inflection point in revenues for the company. Management expects that in 2028, the interstate I-70 interchange will be completed, and commercial land will start being developed. To which the CEO, Mark Harding, said:

“we're really going to see, we're going to see delivery of lots on the residential side, and then we think we double up on that revenue stream on the delivery lots on the commercial side.”

Another positive from the last earnings call is that management is focusing more on land acquisition as opposed to more water reservoir acquisition, and it will only invest in more reservoirs if they are adjacent to the current ones. I think this is the right decision, since the company still has a large water volume available, and will now focus on developing land to serve its reserves. One of the topics I mentioned in my last article was that management stated it was developing land and building houses, but intended to sell it in the future to acquire more water reservoirs. I view that negatively since it would turn a cash-generating asset into a noncash-generating asset, or, push the return on investment further away. So, by focusing on land acquisition, the company continues to drive revenue from selling finished lots to home builders, building more rental homes, receiving connection fees, and accelerating the recurring water revenue. But also, by choosing to develop land, it also improves the value of the whole adjacent community, making it more interesting for commercial projects and long-term investment.

Financials

Total revenue for the three months ended 30 NOV 2025, increased 58.8% YoY, from $5.7 million to $9.1 million, but it was largely affected by non-recurring lot sales revenue, which is influenced by the recognition of Sky Ranch phases' completion. Removing the non-recurring revenue, total revenue declined about 9% YoY, largely due to lower oil & gas water usage. Cost of revenue increased 38% YoY, from $2.1 million to $2.9 million, again, largely due to lot development cost recognition. General and administrative expenses actually reduced YoY, from $1.8 million to $1.7 million. Within the other income (expense), the highlight was the lower oil & gas royalty income, from $2.8 million to $0.7 million, which was expected, but it is something that may increase as these new wells seem to be more efficient, operational at the current oil prices. Net income increased 16% YoY, from $3.9 million to $4.5 million.

The company has a strong balance sheet, with total assets at $168 million, while total liabilities are $21 million. The company has $17 million in cash, while its debt is $7.5 million. PCYO also has $51.8 million in notes receivable, which are reimbursements from building public infrastructure.

Valuation

On valuation metrics, PCYO is being valued above its peers in the utilities sector, with EV/Sales of 9.36x vs. sector median of 4.63x, and EV/EBITDA of 21.77x vs sector median of 20.52x. However, considering that the company still has those $51.8 million in notes receivable and the growth acceleration coming from commercials in 2028, according to management expectations, but also for the next quarters, with the return of oil & gas production bringing back important water revenue. I think PCYO could be traded higher.

Risk

PCYO’s growth depends on execution. Land development is gradual, and it exposes the company to Denver’s housing market cycles. Oil & gas revenues are volatile, and there is no guarantee that oil & gas production will continue. Lastly, delays in the I-70 interchange would postpone commercial development, delaying the expected revenue, potentially making the stock appear expensive in the meantime.

Conclusion

So, in my view, momentum is returning with these new wells, recurring water revenue is getting more significant with more houses built, and the management plan of building for its water reserves makes more sense to me than the previous plan. For now, I view the stock as a “Buy”.

data.title: Fashionably Late Cycle: The S&P 500 Market Leaders Index
data.description:
By Elizabeth Bebb

The late-cycle economy is defined as the final phase of the macroeconomic cycle when economic activity hits its peak. The period is characterized by high but slowing growth, rising inflation that may affect profit generation, a tight labor market and volatility in interest rates.

Given these conditions, many market participants are considering how best to approach diversification to navigate an environment characterized by heightened market risk and uncertainty around future corporate profits and economic growth. With the S&P 500® currently highly concentrated in a few sectors and stocks, addressing these challenges can involve a range of strategies—from broad asset allocation to more selective stock picking.

In this blog, we examine the S&P 500 Market Leaders Index as a potential strategic solution. Launched on Nov. 11, 2024, this index tracks leading companies within The 500® as identified by their consistently high free cash flow margins, strong returns on invested capital and significant market share.

In 2025, The 500 recorded strong performance, driven by ongoing momentum. The S&P 500 Market Leaders Index also performed well compared to its benchmark. Over the long-term back-tested period, the S&P 500 Market Leaders Index delivered strong results, outperforming The 500 both in absolute terms and on a risk-adjusted basis (see Exhibit 1).

Additionally, the index has historically outperformed The 500 during most market downturns while showing lower volatility and a reduced downside capture ratio. This performance can be especially notable in the late stages of the economic cycle, when markets are more prone to stress events.

Fundamentally, the S&P 500 Market Leaders Index is most similar to a traditional quality strategy—tracking constituents that tend to demonstrate higher margins, strong returns on capital and lower leverage (see Exhibit 2). The strategy’s significant emphasis on higher profitability may render it more defensive than other quality strategies because the most profitable stocks typically have a larger “margin of safety,” allowing them to withstand economic shocks or slowing growth while remaining profitable.

The focus on high market share bolsters the index’s overall defensive characteristics, as companies with substantial market share typically benefit from competitive advantages that establish a defensive “moat” around their business models. Tangible benefits of a defensive moat may include pricing power (allowing higher cost inflation to be passed through to pricing), lower sourcing costs and the ability to leverage economies of scale or network effects to sustain revenue, thereby enabling companies to remain profitable throughout the business cycle.

Quality characteristics may resonate more in some sectors than others. Exhibit 3 shows that historically the highest overweight has been in the Information Technology and Consumer Staples sectors. However, the current weight in Financials is much higher than it has been historically, reducing the underweight in the sector.

The S&P 500 Market Leaders Index includes 5 of the Magnificent 7 companies (it does not contain Amazon or Tesla). These companies are distributed across Information Technology, Consumer Services and Communication Services. The total weight of these constituents in the index is 22.7%; however, the S&P 500 benchmark universe holds a 34.9% weight.

The S&P 500 Market Leaders Index’s strong outperformance in periods of economic stress is empirically demonstrated in the back-tested period, where the index outperformed The 500 across all macroeconomic environments—most significantly in falling growth environments.

The S&P 500 Market Leaders Index may provide a source of diversification relative to the benchmark universe, potentially providing a framework for navigating late-cycle market conditions.

Disclosure: Copyright © 2026 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit www.spdji.com. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
